blocks.body: First Conditional shows a real or likely future situation with if + present, will + base verb.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"Use the First Conditional when the condition is real or possible and the result belongs to the future.","rules":[{"label":"REAL POSSIBILITY","color":"emerald","text":"The if-clause stays in Present Simple, and the result often uses will + base verb.","example":"If it rains, we'll stay at home."},{"label":"WARNING OR PROMISE","color":"blue","text":"The structure often sounds like a prediction, promise, warning, or planned reaction.","example":"If you study, you'll pass the exam."},{"label":"NO WILL AFTER IF","color":"amber","text":"In a normal if-clause, future meaning is already clear without will.","example":"If she calls, I'll answer."}]}

{"title":"1. Form","intro":"The most typical pattern is if + present simple, will + base verb.","items":[{"label":"Main pattern","title":"if + present, will + V","subtitle":"If we leave now, we will catch the train."},{"label":"If-clause first","title":"If + clause, result","subtitle":"If she studies, she will feel more confident."},{"label":"Result first","title":"result + if + clause","subtitle":"I will call you if I arrive late."},{"label":"Other results","title":"may / might / can / imperative","subtitle":"If you finish early, you can go home."}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"Possible future result","color":"emerald","description":"The condition is realistic, so the result also feels possible.","examples":[{"en":"If the weather improves, we'll go for a walk.","ua":"If the weather improves, we'll go for a walk."},{"en":"If you don't hurry, you'll miss the bus.","ua":"If you don't hurry, you'll miss the bus."}]},{"label":"Warning, promise, decision","color":"blue","description":"We often talk about a result we expect or control.","examples":[{"en":"If you help me now, I'll help you later.","ua":"If you help me now, I'll help you later."},{"en":"If they arrive early, we'll make coffee.","ua":"If they arrive early, we'll make coffee."}]},{"label":"Not only with will","color":"amber","description":"The main clause can also use may, might, can, or the imperative.","examples":[{"en":"If you finish this section, you can take a break.","ua":"If you finish this section, you can take a break."},{"en":"If Anna calls, tell her I'm in a meeting.","ua":"If Anna calls, tell her I'm in a meeting."}]}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Putting will in the if-clause.","wrong":"If it will rain, we'll stay at home.","right":"If it rains, we'll stay at home.","hint":"After if, a normal First Conditional uses the present simple."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Mixing up First and Zero Conditional.","wrong":"If you press this key tomorrow, the file opens.","right":"If you press this key tomorrow, the file will open.","hint":"A specific future result usually needs the First Conditional."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using to after will.","wrong":"If she arrives early, we will to start on time.","right":"If she arrives early, we will start on time.","hint":"After will, use the base verb without to."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["Use if + present simple, will + base verb for a real future possibility.","After if, we do not normally put will.","The result can also use may, might, can, or the imperative.","The First Conditional describes a specific future result, not a general rule."]}
